title: Finding and repairing a garden pond liner leak
subtitle: Locating the puncture in a butyl or EPDM liner and patching it without draining the pond
excerpt: A garden pond liner leak causes a steady drop in water level that stabilises when the water surface reaches the level of the hole. Locating the puncture involves waiting for the level to stabilise, then working along the liner at that level to find the damaged area. Butyl and EPDM liners can be repaired with a compatible self-adhesive patch while damp.
sourceNotes: RHS guide to pond construction and maintenance. Bradshaws pond supplies repair guidance for butyl and EPDM. Pond Plant World leaking pond diagnosis guide. CIRIA good practice guide on sustainable drainage for surface water management (for context on liner specification).

content.text: A pond liner leak causes the water level to drop and then stabilise. The stabilisation point is the level of the hole: water cannot drain below the puncture. If the level drops to the overflow or to the planted shelving area and holds there, the hole is at that height. If the pond drains completely, the hole is below the deepest planted area.
Confirming it is a liner leak and not evaporation

A pond losing 10-25 mm of water per day in hot weather is evaporating normally. A liner leak loses more consistently regardless of temperature and does not recover after rain (rain adds water but the leak rate exceeds rainfall on still days). Place a container of water next to the pond. If both drop at the same daily rate, it is evaporation. If the pond drops faster, it is a leak.
Stop topping up the pond and let the water level drop until it stabilises. Note the height.
Clear marginal plants and gravel from the liner around the perimeter at the stabilised water level.

Run your hand along the liner at that level, feeling for bubbles, soft spots, or the slight raised lip of a puncture. In bright sunlight, sight along the liner at a low angle. A small reflective distortion can reveal the hole.
If you cannot find it by touch, lower the water level below the stabilisation point. Look for a small damp spot on the liner that persists after the surrounding liner dries.
Clean the liner around the hole: remove algae, soil, and grit from a 200 mm radius circle around the puncture. Pat dry with a cloth.
Cut a patch from the repair kit material: round or oval, at least 100 mm in diameter (patch must overlap the hole by at least 50 mm in all directions).

Peel the backing and press the self-adhesive patch firmly onto the dry liner, working from the centre outward to avoid air bubbles. The liner need not be bone dry: most butyl and EPDM patches bond on damp surfaces, but standing water on the repair area will prevent adhesion.
Press firmly for 2-3 minutes. Allow 2-4 hours of cure time before refilling the pond.

Butyl liners become brittle after 20-30 years, particularly above the waterline where UV causes oxidation. If you find multiple holes close together, or if the liner crumbles when you handle it, patching is not effective. A full liner replacement is the only lasting solution.
